41. Write @ -
Input
{
  "content": "# Kubernetes API Server Architecture and Extension Guide\n\n## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies\n\n### What the apiserver library is\n\nThe `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/` library provides a reusable framework for building Kubernetes-style API servers. It is not the kube-apiserver itself; rather, kube-apiserver is one consumer of this library. The library handles HTTP serving, request routing, authentication, authorization, admission control, storage abstraction, watch/notification, content negotiation, API discovery, and graceful lifecycle management.\n\nAny Go program that needs to serve a Kubernetes-compatible REST API (with support for watches, server-side apply, declarative validation, etc.) can import this library and build on it. The `apiextensions-apiserver` (serving CRDs) and `kube-aggregator` (the aggregation layer) are two other major consumers that ship in the same binary as kube-apiserver.\n\n### Boundary: what lives inside vs. outside\n\n**Inside `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`:**\n- Generic server lifecycle (`pkg/server/`)\n- Request filter chain (authn, authz, audit, flow control, timeout, etc.)\n- REST endpoint installation and HTTP verb mapping (`pkg/endpoints/`)\n- Storage abstraction and etcd3 backend (`pkg/storage/`)\n- Watch cache (`pkg/storage/cacher/`)\n- Admission control interfaces and built-in plugins (`pkg/admission/`)\n- Authentication and authorization interfaces (`pkg/authentication/`, `pkg/authorization/`)\n- OpenAPI schema generation and serving (`pkg/endpoints/openapi/`)\n- Server-sent encryption at rest (`pkg/storage/value/`)\n\n**Outside (upstream dependencies):**\n- `k8s.io/apimachinery` -- runtime types, scheme, serialization, versioning, field selectors, labels. The apiserver library never modifies apimachinery types.\n- `k8s.io/client-go` -- used for the loopback client, informers, leader election. The apiserver creates a privileged loopback client for internal controllers.\n- `go-restful` -- HTTP routing framework for API group endpoints.\n- `etcd` (client library `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3`) -- the only supported durable storage backend.\n- `go.opentelemetry.io/otel` -- distributed tracing.\n\n**Outside (downstream consumers):**\n- `cmd/kube-apiserver/` -- wires up three delegation layers and all built-in API groups.\n- `pkg/controlplane/` -- the kube-apiserver-specific control plane that registers built-in resources (pods, services, deployments, etc.).\n- `staging/src/k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver/` -- serves CustomResourceDefinitions.\n- `staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator/` -- the front proxy that routes to aggregated API servers.\n\n### The delegation chain\n\nA running kube-apiserver is not a single `GenericAPIServer` -- it is a chain of three, composed via the `DelegationTarget` interface:\n\n```\naggregatorServer  -->  kubeAPIServer  -->  apiExtensionsServer  -->  emptyDelegate\n```\n\nThis chain is built in `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go` in `CreateServerChain()`:\n\n```go\napiExtensionsServer, err := config.ApiExtensions.New(\n    genericapiserver.NewEmptyDelegateWithCustomHandler(notFoundHandler))\nkubeAPIServer, err := config.KubeAPIs.New(apiExtensionsServer.GenericAPIServer)\naggregatorServer, err := controlplaneapiserver.CreateAggregatorServer(\n    config.Aggregator, kubeAPIServer.ControlPlane.GenericAPIServer, ...)\n```\n\nThe `DelegationTarget` interface (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:312`) allows each server to contribute its own:\n- `UnprotectedHandler()` -- served behind the director for its registered paths\n- `PostStartHooks()` / `PreShutdownHooks()` -- aggregated and run once by the outermost server\n- Health checks, `MuxAndDiscoveryCompleteSignals`\n\nWhen a request arrives, the director (see Section 3) tries each server's registered paths. If no match is found in the aggregator or kube-apiserver, the request falls through to the apiextensions-apiserver (which handles CRD-based resources), and finally to a `notFoundHandler`.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Key Components and Their Responsibilities\n\n### 2.1 GenericAPIServer\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:110`\n\nThe central type. Each layer in the delegation chain holds one. Key fields:\n\n| Field | Purpose |\n|-------|---------|\n| `Handler` | The `APIServerHandler` that actually routes HTTP |\n| `delegationTarget` | The next server in the chain |\n| `admissionControl` | Admission plugin chain |\n| `Authorizer` | Authorization decision maker |\n| `AuditBackend` | Where audit events go |\n| `postStartHooks` | Map of named hooks, run after listener starts |\n| `preShutdownHooks` | Map of named hooks, run during shutdown |\n| `lifecycleSignals` | Channels that track shutdown progress |\n| `LoopbackClientConfig` | Privileged config for calling back into self |\n| `SecureServingInfo` | TLS listener configuration |\n\n### 2.2 Config and CompletedConfig\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/config.go:112`\n\n`Config` holds everything needed to construct a `GenericAPIServer`. Notable fields:\n- `SecureServing` -- TLS listener config\n- `Authentication` / `Authorization` -- pluggable authn/authz\n- `AdmissionControl` -- the admission chain\n- `FlowControl` -- priority and fairness (APF) settings\n- `BuildHandlerChainFunc` -- the function that wraps the core handler with filters (default: `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`)\n- `AuditBackend` / `AuditPolicyRuleEvaluator` -- audit logging\n- `FeatureGate` -- feature gate plumbing\n- `EffectiveVersion` -- controls which API versions are served\n\n`CompletedConfig.New()` (`config.go:779`) is where the server is actually instantiated. It:\n1. Creates the `GenericAPIServer` struct\n2. Installs the delegation chain\n3. Registers PostStartHooks for shared informers and APF bootstrap\n4. Calls `BuildHandlerChainFunc` to construct the filter chain\n\n### 2.3 APIServerHandler and the Director\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/handler.go:42`\n\n```go\ntype APIServerHandler struct {\n    FullHandlerChain   http.Handler        // the full filter chain\n    GoRestfulContainer *restful.Container  // API group routes\n    NonGoRestfulMux    *mux.PathRecorderMux // non-API routes (healthz, metrics, etc.)\n    Director           http.Handler        // routes between the two\n}\n```\n\nThe `director` (`handler.go:121`) checks if the request path is registered in the `GoRestfulContainer`. If so, the request is served there. Otherwise, it falls to the `NonGoRestfulMux`. This separation exists because API group endpoints (e.g., `/apis/apps/v1/deployments`) are registered dynamically via go-restful, while system endpoints (`/healthz`, `/metrics`, `/debug/pprof`) are registered statically on the mux.\n\n`FullHandlerChain` wraps the `director` with the full filter chain (authentication, authorization, audit, etc.). Every incoming request enters through `FullHandlerChain`.\n\n### 2.4 APIGroupInfo and API Installation\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:72`\n\n```go\ntype APIGroupInfo struct {\n    PrioritizedVersions            []schema.GroupVersion\n    VersionedResourcesStorageMap   map[string]map[string]rest.Storage\n    Scheme                         *runtime.Scheme\n    NegotiatedSerializer           runtime.NegotiatedSerializer\n    ParameterCodec                 runtime.ParameterCodec\n}\n```\n\n`VersionedResourcesStorageMap` is a two-level map: `version -> resourceName -> rest.Storage`. When an API group is installed (via `GenericAPIServer.InstallAPIGroup` or `InstallLegacyAPIGroup`), the server iterates this map and calls `APIInstaller.Install()` (`pkg/endpoints/installer.go:195`) for each version. The installer:\n\n1. Sorts resource paths deterministically\n2. For each path, calls `registerResourceHandlers()` which inspects the `rest.Storage` object to determine which interfaces it implements (`rest.Getter`, `rest.Lister`, `rest.Creater`, etc.)\n3. Creates go-restful `Route` objects for each supported HTTP verb\n4. Registers the routes on a go-restful `WebService`\n\nThis is the mechanism by which the REST verbs available for a resource are determined: if the storage object implements `rest.Updater`, PUT is registered; if it implements `rest.Watcher`, the watch endpoint is registered; and so on.\n\n### 2.5 REST Storage Interfaces\n\n**File:** `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go`\n\nThe REST layer defines fine-grained interfaces that a resource storage can implement:\n\n| Interface | Verb | Method |\n|-----------|------|--------|\n| `Getter` | GET | `Get(ctx, name, options)` |\n| `Lister` | LIST | `List(ctx, options)` |\n| `Creater` | POST | `Create(ctx, obj, validate, options)` |\n| `Updater` | PUT | `Update(ctx, name, objInfo, validate, validate, options)` |\n| `Patcher` | PATCH | (composed from Get + Update) |\n| `GracefulDeleter` | DELETE | `Delete(ctx, name, validate, options)` |\n| `CollectionDeleter` | DELETE collection | `DeleteCollection(ctx, validate, options, listOptions)` |\n| `Watcher` | WATCH | `Watch(ctx, options)` |\n| `Connecter` | CONNECT | `Connect(ctx, name, options, responder)` |\n\n`StandardStorage` aggregates `Getter`, `Lister`, `CreaterUpdater`, `GracefulDeleter`, and `Watcher` into a single convenience interface.\n\n### 2.6 Generic Registry Store\n\n**File:** `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go:100`\n\nThe `Store` struct is the standard implementation of `rest.StandardStorage`. Almost every built-in Kubernetes resource (pods, deployments, services, etc.) uses an embedded `Store`. It provides:\n\n- CRUD operations with conflict detection via `ResourceVersion`\n- Pluggable strategies: `CreateStrategy`, `UpdateStrategy`, `DeleteStrategy` (implement resource-specific validation, defaulting, canonicalization)\n- Lifecycle hooks: `BeginCreate`, `AfterCreate`, `BeginUpdate`, `AfterUpdate`, `AfterDelete`\n- `Decorator` for transforming objects after storage retrieval\n- `TTLFunc` for time-to-live eviction (used by Events)\n- `Storage` field holding a `DryRunnableStorage` that wraps the underlying `storage.Interface`\n- `DeleteCollectionWorkers` for parallel bulk deletion\n- Garbage collection integration via finalizer awareness\n\nThe Store delegates actual persistence to a `storage.Interface` (`pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`), which is typically the cacher wrapping etcd3.\n\n### 2.7 Storage Interface and etcd3 Backend\n\n**File:** `pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`\n\n```go\ntype Interface interface {\n    Versioner() Versioner\n    Create(ctx, key, obj, out, ttl) error\n    Delete(ctx, key, out, preconditions, validateDeletion, cachedExistingObject) error\n    Watch(ctx, key, opts) (watch.Interface, error)\n    Get(ctx, key, opts, objPtr) error\n    GetList(ctx, key, opts, listObj) error\n    GuaranteedUpdate(ctx, key, destination, ignoreNotFound, preconditions, tryUpdate, cachedExistingObject) error\n    Count(key) (int64, error)\n    ReadinessCheck() error\n    RequestWatchProgress(ctx) error\n}\n```\n\nThe `etcd3.store` (`pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go`) implements this interface. It:\n- Serializes objects to protobuf or JSON before writing to etcd\n- Uses etcd transactions for conflict detection (`GuaranteedUpdate` performs a compare-and-swap)\n- Manages a `leaseManager` for efficient TTL handling (multiplexes many objects onto shared etcd leases)\n- Implements `Watch` by creating etcd watchers with revision-based resumption\n- Handles encryption at rest via `value.Transformer` (wraps the codec layer)\n\n### 2.8 Watch Cache (Cacher)\n\n**File:** `pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go:80`\n\nThe `Cacher` sits between the generic registry and the etcd3 store. It implements `storage.Interface` and serves as an in-memory read cache and watch multiplexer. Key behaviors:\n\n- **Read path:** `Get` and `GetList` are served from the in-memory cache, avoiding etcd round-trips for most reads. The cache is populated by a reflector that watches etcd.\n- **Watch path:** Client watches are served from the in-memory event buffer rather than creating individual etcd watchers. This is critical for scalability -- without it, every `kubectl get pods -w` would create a separate etcd watch.\n- **Event history:** The `watchCache` (`pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go`) maintains a ring buffer of recent events (`DefaultEventFreshDuration` is about 75 seconds). Clients that reconnect with a recent `resourceVersion` can resume from this buffer without a full relist.\n- **Bookmark events:** Sent periodically (every ~60s, `defaultBookmarkFrequency`) to advance clients' `resourceVersion` without delivering actual changes, reducing the chance of `410 Gone` on reconnect.\n- **Consistency:** The cache may be slightly stale for reads. Writes always go to etcd first. A List with `resourceVersion=\"\"` (the default) is served from cache and may not reflect the most recent write. A List with `resourceVersion=\"0\"` explicitly requests the latest cached state. A Get/List with a specific `resourceVersion` will wait until the cache has caught up to that version (with a timeout).\n\nConfig fields (`cacher.go:80`):\n- `Storage` -- the underlying `storage.Interface` (etcd3 store)\n- `ResourcePrefix` -- etcd key prefix for this resource type\n- `KeyFunc`, `GetAttrsFunc` -- extract storage keys and indexable attributes\n- `IndexerFuncs` / `Indexers` -- for optimizing which watchers receive which events\n- `EventsHistoryWindow` -- minimum duration the event buffer covers\n\n### 2.9 Admission Control\n\n**File:** `pkg/admission/interfaces.go`\n\nAdmission runs after authentication and authorization but before the object is persisted. Two interfaces:\n\n```go\ntype MutationInterface interface {\n    Interface\n    Admit(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error\n}\n\ntype ValidationInterface interface {\n    Interface\n    Validate(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error\n}\n```\n\nThe `Attributes` interface (`interfaces.go:31`) provides request context to plugins: the operation type (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, CONNECT), the object, the old object (for updates), the user info, the resource, and the subresource.\n\nThe admission chain runs mutation plugins first, then validation plugins. Mutation plugins may modify the object; validation plugins must not. A request that fails any validation plugin is rejected. The chain is constructed at server startup and cannot be modified at runtime.\n\nBuilt-in plugin categories under `pkg/admission/plugin/`:\n- **webhook/** -- `mutating/` and `validating/` webhook dispatchers that call out to external admission webhooks\n- **policy/** -- ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL-based in-process validation)\n- **cel/** -- CEL compilation and evaluation\n- **namespace/** -- namespace lifecycle enforcement (e.g., rejecting operations in terminating namespaces)\n- **resourcequota/** -- resource quota enforcement\n\n### 2.10 Authentication and Authorization\n\n**Authentication** (`pkg/authentication/`):\n- `authenticator.Request` interface: `AuthenticateRequest(req *http.Request) (*Response, bool, error)`\n- Implementations: bearer token (`request/bearertoken/`), x509 client certs (`request/x509/`), anonymous (`request/anonymous/`), request header proxy (`request/headerrequest/`), websocket (`request/websocket/`)\n- Token authenticators: JWT (`token/tokenfile/`, `token/cache/`), union (tries multiple authenticators in sequence)\n- The authentication filter (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go`) calls into the configured authenticator and attaches user info to the request context\n\n**Authorization** (`pkg/authorization/`):\n- `authorizer.Authorizer` interface: `Authorize(ctx, Attributes) (Decision, reason, error)`\n- The authorization filter (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go`) checks the authorizer after authentication succeeds\n- Decisions: `DecisionAllow`, `DecisionDeny`, `DecisionNoOpinion`\n\n### 2.11 Priority and Fairness (APF)\n\n**Files:** `pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go`, `pkg/util/flowcontrol/`\n\nAPF replaces the older `MaxInFlightLimit` filter. It classifies requests into flow schemas and priority levels, then applies fair queuing and concurrency limiting. In `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`, if `FlowControl` is configured, the APF filter is used; otherwise, the simpler `MaxInFlightLimit` filter is used.\n\nAPF is significant operationally because it prevents a single misbehaving client from starving the server. Requests that exceed their queue depth receive `429 Too Many Requests` or `503 Service Unavailable` responses.\n\n---\n\n## 3. End-to-End Request Flow\n\nThis section traces a `CREATE Pod` request from the TCP connection to etcd write and response.\n\n### 3.1 TLS Termination and Handler Chain Entry\n\nThe request arrives at the TLS listener configured by `SecureServingInfo`. It enters `APIServerHandler.FullHandlerChain`, which is the outermost `http.Handler` wrapping all filters.\n\n### 3.2 Filter Chain\n\nThe filter chain is constructed by `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` (`pkg/server/config.go:1028`). Filters are applied inside-out in the source code, so the first filter listed at the bottom of the function runs first. The execution order, from outermost to innermost:\n\n1. **AuditInit** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit_init.go`) -- initializes the audit context on the request\n2. **PanicRecovery** (`pkg/server/filters/wrap.go`) -- catches panics, logs them, returns 500\n3. **MuxAndDiscoveryComplete** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/muxcomplete.go`) -- blocks requests until all API groups are installed (returns 503 during startup)\n4. **RequestInfo** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/requestinfo.go`) -- parses the URL into API group, version, resource, namespace, name, verb; attaches to context\n5. **RequestReceivedTimestamp** -- records when the request was received\n6. **LatencyTrackers** -- sets up latency tracking in the context\n7. **HTTPLogging** -- request/response logging\n8. **RetryAfter** (`pkg/server/filters/retry-after.go`) -- if the server is shutting down and `ShutdownSendRetryAfter` is enabled, returns `429` with `Retry-After` header\n9. **HSTS** -- adds Strict-Transport-Security headers\n10. **CacheControl** -- adds Cache-Control headers\n11. **GoawayChance** (`pkg/server/filters/goaway.go`) -- randomly sends GOAWAY to HTTP/2 clients to spread reconnections (avoids thundering herd on server restart)\n12. **WatchTermination** -- ensures watches are terminated during shutdown\n13. **WaitGroup** (`pkg/server/filters/waitgroup.go`) -- adds the request to the server's wait group so shutdown can wait for in-flight requests\n14. **RequestDeadline** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/request_deadline.go`) -- sets a context deadline based on the request timeout parameter\n15. **TimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests** (`pkg/server/filters/timeout.go`) -- enforces a hard timeout on non-long-running requests (watches, proxy, exec are long-running)\n16. **WarningRecorder** -- attaches a warning recorder to context for collecting API warnings\n17. **CORS** (`pkg/server/filters/cors.go`) -- handles Cross-Origin Resource Sharing\n18. **Authentication** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go`) -- calls the authenticator; on failure, returns 401\n19. **Tracing** -- OpenTelemetry distributed tracing\n20. **Audit** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit.go`) -- records the audit event (RequestReceived stage)\n21. **Impersonation** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/impersonation/`) -- handles user impersonation headers\n22. **FlowControl** (`pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go`) -- APF queuing and concurrency limiting (or `MaxInFlightLimit` if APF is disabled)\n23. **Authorization** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go`) -- calls the authorizer; on failure, returns 403\n\n### 3.3 Director Routing\n\nAfter the filter chain, the request reaches the `director` (`pkg/server/handler.go:121`). For a `POST /api/v1/namespaces/default/pods`, the director finds this path is registered in the `GoRestfulContainer` and dispatches there.\n\n### 3.4 go-restful Routing to REST Handler\n\nThe go-restful container holds `WebService` instances registered during API installation. Each `WebService` covers a URL prefix like `/api/v1` or `/apis/apps/v1`. The go-restful router matches the request to a specific `Route`, which points to a handler function created by `registerResourceHandlers` in `pkg/endpoints/installer.go`.\n\n### 3.5 Request Handler (Create)\n\nFor a CREATE operation, the handler is `createHandler` (`pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go`). This function:\n\n1. **Decodes** the request body using the negotiated serializer (JSON, YAML, or protobuf)\n2. **Defaults** the object using the scheme's defaulting functions\n3. Calls **admission** (mutation + validation)\n4. Calls the `rest.Storage`'s `Create` method\n\n### 3.6 Generic Registry Store.Create\n\nThe `Store.Create()` method in `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go`:\n\n1. Calls `BeginCreate` hook (if set)\n2. Runs `CreateStrategy.PrepareForCreate()` -- resource-specific defaulting (e.g., setting pod status to Pending)\n3. Runs `CreateStrategy.Validate()` -- resource-specific validation\n4. Computes the storage key using `KeyFunc`\n5. Calls `Storage.Create()` on the underlying `storage.Interface`\n6. Runs `AfterCreate` hook (if set)\n7. Runs `Decorator` (if set)\n\n### 3.7 Cacher Pass-Through on Write\n\nFor write operations (Create, Update, Delete), the `Cacher` passes through directly to the underlying etcd3 store. The cacher does not cache writes -- they always go to etcd. The cacher's reflector will pick up the change asynchronously and update the in-memory cache.\n\n### 3.8 etcd3 Store.Create\n\nThe `etcd3.store.Create()` method (`pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go`):\n\n1. Serializes the object using the codec (applies `value.Transformer` for encryption at rest)\n2. Computes the etcd key (e.g., `/registry/pods/default/my-pod`)\n3. Executes an etcd `Txn` (transaction): compare that the key does not exist, then put. If the key exists, returns `AlreadyExists` error.\n4. Deserializes the response (including the new `resourceVersion` from the etcd `ModRevision`)\n5. Returns the created object with its `resourceVersion` set\n\n### 3.9 Response Serialization\n\nThe response flows back up through the handler chain. The REST handler serializes the response object using content negotiation (the client's `Accept` header determines JSON, YAML, or protobuf). The response passes back out through all filters (audit records the ResponseComplete stage, latency trackers finalize, etc.).\n\n### 3.10 Watch Delivery\n\nAny active watch on pods in the `default` namespace will be notified. The cacher's reflector observes the etcd change, adds it to the `watchCache` ring buffer, and the `cacheWatcher` goroutines (`pkg/storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go`) deliver the event to connected watchers (filtered by namespace, label selector, field selector).\n\n---\n\n## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs\n\n### 4.1 Watch Cache Consistency\n\nThe watch cache is eventually consistent. A client that creates an object and immediately lists may not see it if the list is served from cache. This is by design -- `resourceVersion=\"\"` means \"serve from cache, any version is acceptable.\"\n\n**Operational implication:** Controllers should use the `resourceVersion` from their watch events, not perform unversioned lists. If a controller must see its own write, it should read the response from the write operation, which contains the authoritative object.\n\n**Risk of `410 Gone`:** If a watcher disconnects for longer than the event buffer window (~75 seconds), and then reconnects with its last `resourceVersion`, the cacher cannot serve the missed events. It returns `410 Gone`, forcing the client to relist. Bookmark events mitigate this by advancing the client's `resourceVersion` more frequently.\n\n### 4.2 etcd Transaction Failures and Conflict Detection\n\nAll writes use etcd transactions with compare-and-swap on the `modRevision`. Concurrent writes to the same key result in one succeeding and the others receiving a `Conflict` (HTTP 409) error. Clients (including controllers) are expected to retry with exponential backoff.\n\n`GuaranteedUpdate` in etcd3 (`pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go`) retries the transaction internally if the `tryUpdate` function returns a modified object, but only a limited number of times. Sustained contention on a hot key (e.g., a frequently-updated ConfigMap) can cause write failures.\n\n### 4.3 Graceful Shutdown Sequence\n\nThe shutdown sequence (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:536`, see ASCII diagram at line 496) is multi-phase:\n\n1. **Context cancellation** triggers `ShutdownInitiated`\n2. **ShutdownDelayDuration** (default 0s, configurable) elapses \u2192 `AfterShutdownDelayDuration`. This delay exists to allow load balancers to remove the server from rotation before it stops accepting requests.\n3. **PreShutdownHooks** execute (e.g., draining leader election)\n4. Both signals above merge into `NotAcceptingNewRequest`\n5. Two wait groups drain in parallel: `NonLongRunningRequestWaitGroup` and `WatchRequestWaitGroup`\n6. Both drained \u2192 `InFlightRequestsDrained`\n7. HTTP server stop (`stopHttpServerCtx`) and audit backend shutdown happen\n8. `HTTPServerStoppedListening` fires\n\n**Operational implication:** Setting `ShutdownDelayDuration` too low in a load-balanced environment can cause client errors during rolling restarts. Too high delays node shutdown.\n\n### 4.4 Priority and Fairness vs. MaxInFlight\n\nAPF (`FlowControl`) supersedes the simpler `MaxInFlightLimit` / `MaxMutatingInFlightLimit` settings. When APF is enabled, those limits are ignored.\n\nAPF provides multi-tenant fairness: requests from different users/service-accounts are queued independently. However, APF adds memory overhead (queues), latency (queuing time), and configuration complexity (FlowSchema + PriorityLevelConfiguration CRDs).\n\n**Failure mode:** If APF is misconfigured (e.g., the `catch-all` FlowSchema has a very low concurrency share), system-critical requests (like leader election renewals) can be starved. The `exempt` priority level should always have sufficient concurrency for system requests.\n\n### 4.5 Timeout Behavior\n\n`TimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests` (`pkg/server/filters/timeout.go`) wraps non-long-running requests with a hard deadline (default: 60 seconds, from `Config.RequestTimeout`). If the handler doesn't complete in time, the filter:\n1. Writes a `504 Gateway Timeout` response\n2. Does NOT cancel the handler's context -- the handler may continue executing (and the storage operation may complete in etcd), but the client will have received the timeout error\n\n**Operational implication:** A client may receive a timeout error even though the operation succeeded in etcd. Clients must be prepared for this and should check whether the object exists before retrying creates (or use `fieldManager` for idempotent applies).\n\n### 4.6 GoAway Filter and Connection Spreading\n\nThe `GoawayChance` filter (`pkg/server/filters/goaway.go`) randomly sends HTTP/2 GOAWAY frames to clients. This exists because HTTP/2 connections are long-lived, and without it, all clients would stay pinned to the same server after a rolling restart. The filter probabilistically forces clients to reconnect, spreading load.\n\n**Tradeoff:** Setting `GoawayChance` too high causes excessive reconnection overhead. Too low, and connection spreading is ineffective after a restart.\n\n### 4.7 Admission Webhook Failures\n\nExternal admission webhooks are called over HTTPS. If a webhook is unreachable or times out:\n- `failurePolicy: Fail` (default for validating webhooks) -- the request is rejected\n- `failurePolicy: Ignore` -- the request proceeds without the webhook's check\n\n**Operational implication:** A misconfigured validating webhook with `failurePolicy: Fail` that matches all resources (including its own namespace) can deadlock the cluster: the webhook's own pod cannot be updated because the webhook is down.\n\n### 4.8 Storage Versioning and Forward/Backward Compatibility\n\nObjects are stored in etcd at a specific \"storage version\" (the version used to serialize to etcd). When the storage version changes across upgrades, old objects remain in the old format until rewritten. The `StorageVersionMigrator` handles this.\n\nThe `EffectiveVersion` and `EmulationForwardCompatible` fields on `Config` control which API versions are served. This allows a newer binary to emulate an older version's API surface.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Extension Points, Hooks, and Their Risks\n\n### 5.1 BuildHandlerChainFunc\n\n**Location:** `Config.BuildHandlerChainFunc` (`pkg/server/config.go:186`)\n\nThis function signature is `func(apiHandler http.Handler, c *Config) http.Handler`. It completely controls the filter chain. Setting a custom function replaces the entire default chain.\n\n**Use case:** Adding custom middleware (e.g., request logging, custom rate limiting, header injection).\n\n**Risk:** Omitting any default filter (e.g., authentication, authorization) would be a security vulnerability. If you override this, you must replicate or call `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` and wrap its result.\n\n### 5.2 PostStartHooks\n\n**Location:** `GenericAPIServer.postStartHooks`, registered via `AddPostStartHook()` or `AddPostStartHookOrDie()`\n\nPostStartHooks run asynchronously after the HTTP listener starts. They receive a `PostStartHookContext` containing a `LoopbackClientConfig` and `StopCh`.\n\n**Built-in hooks (examples):**\n- `start-informers` -- starts shared informers\n- `priority-and-fairness-config-consumer` -- bootstraps APF configuration\n- `storage-object-count-tracker-hook` -- tracks object counts for APF\n\n**Use case:** Initializing controllers, waiting for caches to sync, registering API discovery info.\n\n**Risk:** A hook that blocks forever will prevent the server from reporting as ready (the readyz check for PostStartHooks will fail). Hooks should respect `StopCh` and not deadlock.\n\n### 5.3 PreShutdownHooks\n\nRun during graceful shutdown. Used for draining leader elections, closing connections to external systems.\n\n### 5.4 Admission Plugins\n\n**Registration:** `pkg/admission/` provides `Register()` for in-process plugins, and the webhook infrastructure for out-of-process plugins.\n\n**Extension model:** You can add custom in-process admission plugins by implementing `MutationInterface` and/or `ValidationInterface` and registering them in the admission chain at startup.\n\n**Risk:** A slow or panicking admission plugin can block or crash request processing. Mutation plugins that make external calls add latency to every write.\n\n### 5.5 REST Storage Interfaces (Custom Resources)\n\n**Location:** `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go`\n\nAny type that implements `rest.Storage` (and optionally `rest.Getter`, `rest.Lister`, etc.) can be registered as an API resource. This is how CRDs work: the apiextensions-apiserver creates dynamic `rest.Storage` implementations backed by unstructured storage.\n\n**Extension model:** To add a custom resource type without CRDs, implement the relevant `rest.` interfaces and register via `APIGroupInfo.VersionedResourcesStorageMap`.\n\n### 5.6 Store Strategy Hooks\n\n**Location:** `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go`\n\nThe `Store` struct exposes several hook points:\n- `CreateStrategy` / `UpdateStrategy` / `DeleteStrategy` -- control validation, defaulting, field clearing\n- `BeginCreate` / `BeginUpdate` -- \"transaction-like\" hooks that return a `FinishFunc` called on completion\n- `AfterCreate` / `AfterUpdate` / `AfterDelete` -- post-operation hooks\n- `Decorator` -- transforms objects before returning to the client (but decorated fields are not watchable)\n\n**Risk:** `BeginCreate`/`BeginUpdate` hooks that return errors prevent the operation. `Decorator` modifications are invisible to watch clients, potentially causing confusing behavior if the decorated data is significant.\n\n### 5.7 Storage Decorators and Encryption\n\n**Location:** `pkg/storage/value/` (encryption transformers), `pkg/server/options/etcd.go` (decorator construction)\n\nStorage decorators wrap the `storage.Interface` to add behavior. The primary decorator is the `Cacher`. Encryption at rest is implemented as a `value.Transformer` applied within the etcd3 store's codec layer.\n\n**Extension model:** Custom `value.Transformer` implementations can add encryption providers (e.g., KMS, AES-CBC, AES-GCM, Secretbox).\n\n### 5.8 Authentication and Authorization Plugins\n\n- Custom `authenticator.Request` implementations can be composed using the union authenticator\n- Custom `authorizer.Authorizer` implementations are set on `Config.Authorization.Authorizer`\n- RBAC, ABAC, webhook, and node authorizers are the built-in implementations\n\n### 5.9 LifecycleSignals\n\n**Location:** `pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go`\n\nSignals like `ShutdownInitiated`, `MuxAndDiscoveryComplete`, `InFlightRequestsDrained`, etc. are `<-chan struct{}` channels. External code can wait on these to coordinate with server lifecycle.\n\n---\n\n## 6. File and Directory Map\n\n### Core Server\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` | GenericAPIServer struct, DelegationTarget, PrepareRun, RunWithContext, lifecycle management |\n| `pkg/server/config.go` | Config, RecommendedConfig, CompletedConfig, DefaultBuildHandlerChain |\n| `pkg/server/handler.go` | APIServerHandler, director routing between GoRestful and NonGoRestful mux |\n| `pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go` | Shutdown lifecycle signal definitions |\n| `pkg/server/hooks.go` | PostStartHook and PreShutdownHook registration |\n| `pkg/server/healthz/` | Health check registration and handlers |\n\n### Request Filters\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go` | Authentication filter |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go` | Authorization filter |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/audit.go` | Audit logging filter |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/impersonation/` | User impersonation |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/requestinfo.go` | URL parsing into API request attributes |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/request_deadline.go` | Per-request deadline enforcement |\n| `pkg/server/filters/maxinflight.go` | Simple max-in-flight concurrency limiter |\n| `pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go` | APF concurrency limiter |\n| `pkg/server/filters/timeout.go` | Non-long-running request timeout |\n| `pkg/server/filters/cors.go` | CORS handling |\n| `pkg/server/filters/goaway.go` | HTTP/2 GOAWAY for connection spreading |\n| `pkg/server/filters/waitgroup.go` | In-flight request tracking for shutdown |\n| `pkg/server/filters/wrap.go` | Panic recovery |\n\n### Endpoint Registration\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/endpoints/installer.go` | APIInstaller -- maps rest.Storage interfaces to HTTP routes |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go` | CREATE request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/update.go` | UPDATE request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/delete.go` | DELETE request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/get.go` | GET/LIST request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/watch.go` | WATCH request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/patch.go` | PATCH request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/fieldmanager/` | Server-side apply field management |\n\n### REST and Registry\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` | All REST storage interfaces (Getter, Lister, Creater, etc.) |\n| `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go` | Generic Store implementing StandardStorage |\n| `pkg/registry/generic/storage_decorator.go` | Storage decorator construction (cacher wrapping) |\n\n### Storage\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/storage/interfaces.go` | storage.Interface -- the low-level storage contract |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go` | etcd3 implementation of storage.Interface |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/watcher.go` | etcd3 watch implementation |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/compact.go` | etcd compaction |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/lease_manager.go` | TTL lease management |\n| `pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go` | In-memory watch cache and read cache |\n| `pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go` | Ring buffer for watch events |\n| `pkg/storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go` | Per-client watch event delivery |\n| `pkg/storage/value/` | Encryption at rest transformers |\n\n### Admission\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/admission/interfaces.go` | MutationInterface, ValidationInterface, Attributes |\n| `pkg/admission/chain.go` | Admission chain execution |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/webhook/mutating/` | Mutating webhook dispatcher |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/webhook/validating/` | Validating webhook dispatcher |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/policy/` | ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL) |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/namespace/` | Namespace lifecycle admission |\n\n### Authentication and Authorization\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/authentication/authenticator/interfaces.go` | authenticator.Request interface |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/bearertoken/` | Bearer token authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/x509/` | Client certificate authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/headerrequest/` | Request header (proxy) authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/anonymous/` | Anonymous authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/token/cache/` | Token authentication response caching |\n| `pkg/authorization/authorizer/interfaces.go` | authorizer.Authorizer interface |\n\n### kube-apiserver Wiring (outside the library, in the consumer)\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go` | CreateServerChain -- builds the three-layer delegation |\n| `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/aggregator.go` | Aggregator layer configuration |\n| `pkg/controlplane/instance.go` | Built-in API group registration (all RESTStorageProviders) |\n| `pkg/controlplane/apiserver/server.go` | Control plane server with PostStartHooks |\n\nAll paths above are relative to `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/` unless they start with `cmd/` or `pkg/controlplane/`, which are at the repository root.\n",
  "file_path": "/workspace/documentation.md"
}
Output
{
  "content": "# Kubernetes API Server Architecture and Extension Guide\n\n## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies\n\n### What the apiserver library is\n\nThe `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/` library provides a reusable framework for building Kubernetes-style API servers. It is not the kube-apiserver itself; rather, kube-apiserver is one consumer of this library. The library handles HTTP serving, request routing, authentication, authorization, admission control, storage abstraction, watch/notification, content negotiation, API discovery, and graceful lifecycle management.\n\nAny Go program that needs to serve a Kubernetes-compatible REST API (with support for watches, server-side apply, declarative validation, etc.) can import this library and build on it. The `apiextensions-apiserver` (serving CRDs) and `kube-aggregator` (the aggregation layer) are two other major consumers that ship in the same binary as kube-apiserver.\n\n### Boundary: what lives inside vs. outside\n\n**Inside `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`:**\n- Generic server lifecycle (`pkg/server/`)\n- Request filter chain (authn, authz, audit, flow control, timeout, etc.)\n- REST endpoint installation and HTTP verb mapping (`pkg/endpoints/`)\n- Storage abstraction and etcd3 backend (`pkg/storage/`)\n- Watch cache (`pkg/storage/cacher/`)\n- Admission control interfaces and built-in plugins (`pkg/admission/`)\n- Authentication and authorization interfaces (`pkg/authentication/`, `pkg/authorization/`)\n- OpenAPI schema generation and serving (`pkg/endpoints/openapi/`)\n- Server-sent encryption at rest (`pkg/storage/value/`)\n\n**Outside (upstream dependencies):**\n- `k8s.io/apimachinery` -- runtime types, scheme, serialization, versioning, field selectors, labels. The apiserver library never modifies apimachinery types.\n- `k8s.io/client-go` -- used for the loopback client, informers, leader election. The apiserver creates a privileged loopback client for internal controllers.\n- `go-restful` -- HTTP routing framework for API group endpoints.\n- `etcd` (client library `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3`) -- the only supported durable storage backend.\n- `go.opentelemetry.io/otel` -- distributed tracing.\n\n**Outside (downstream consumers):**\n- `cmd/kube-apiserver/` -- wires up three delegation layers and all built-in API groups.\n- `pkg/controlplane/` -- the kube-apiserver-specific control plane that registers built-in resources (pods, services, deployments, etc.).\n- `staging/src/k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver/` -- serves CustomResourceDefinitions.\n- `staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator/` -- the front proxy that routes to aggregated API servers.\n\n### The delegation chain\n\nA running kube-apiserver is not a single `GenericAPIServer` -- it is a chain of three, composed via the `DelegationTarget` interface:\n\n```\naggregatorServer  -->  kubeAPIServer  -->  apiExtensionsServer  -->  emptyDelegate\n```\n\nThis chain is built in `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go` in `CreateServerChain()`:\n\n```go\napiExtensionsServer, err := config.ApiExtensions.New(\n    genericapiserver.NewEmptyDelegateWithCustomHandler(notFoundHandler))\nkubeAPIServer, err := config.KubeAPIs.New(apiExtensionsServer.GenericAPIServer)\naggregatorServer, err := controlplaneapiserver.CreateAggregatorServer(\n    config.Aggregator, kubeAPIServer.ControlPlane.GenericAPIServer, ...)\n```\n\nThe `DelegationTarget` interface (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:312`) allows each server to contribute its own:\n- `UnprotectedHandler()` -- served behind the director for its registered paths\n- `PostStartHooks()` / `PreShutdownHooks()` -- aggregated and run once by the outermost server\n- Health checks, `MuxAndDiscoveryCompleteSignals`\n\nWhen a request arrives, the director (see Section 3) tries each server's registered paths. If no match is found in the aggregator or kube-apiserver, the request falls through to the apiextensions-apiserver (which handles CRD-based resources), and finally to a `notFoundHandler`.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Key Components and Their Responsibilities\n\n### 2.1 GenericAPIServer\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:110`\n\nThe central type. Each layer in the delegation chain holds one. Key fields:\n\n| Field | Purpose |\n|-------|---------|\n| `Handler` | The `APIServerHandler` that actually routes HTTP |\n| `delegationTarget` | The next server in the chain |\n| `admissionControl` | Admission plugin chain |\n| `Authorizer` | Authorization decision maker |\n| `AuditBackend` | Where audit events go |\n| `postStartHooks` | Map of named hooks, run after listener starts |\n| `preShutdownHooks` | Map of named hooks, run during shutdown |\n| `lifecycleSignals` | Channels that track shutdown progress |\n| `LoopbackClientConfig` | Privileged config for calling back into self |\n| `SecureServingInfo` | TLS listener configuration |\n\n### 2.2 Config and CompletedConfig\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/config.go:112`\n\n`Config` holds everything needed to construct a `GenericAPIServer`. Notable fields:\n- `SecureServing` -- TLS listener config\n- `Authentication` / `Authorization` -- pluggable authn/authz\n- `AdmissionControl` -- the admission chain\n- `FlowControl` -- priority and fairness (APF) settings\n- `BuildHandlerChainFunc` -- the function that wraps the core handler with filters (default: `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`)\n- `AuditBackend` / `AuditPolicyRuleEvaluator` -- audit logging\n- `FeatureGate` -- feature gate plumbing\n- `EffectiveVersion` -- controls which API versions are served\n\n`CompletedConfig.New()` (`config.go:779`) is where the server is actually instantiated. It:\n1. Creates the `GenericAPIServer` struct\n2. Installs the delegation chain\n3. Registers PostStartHooks for shared informers and APF bootstrap\n4. Calls `BuildHandlerChainFunc` to construct the filter chain\n\n### 2.3 APIServerHandler and the Director\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/handler.go:42`\n\n```go\ntype APIServerHandler struct {\n    FullHandlerChain   http.Handler        // the full filter chain\n    GoRestfulContainer *restful.Container  // API group routes\n    NonGoRestfulMux    *mux.PathRecorderMux // non-API routes (healthz, metrics, etc.)\n    Director           http.Handler        // routes between the two\n}\n```\n\nThe `director` (`handler.go:121`) checks if the request path is registered in the `GoRestfulContainer`. If so, the request is served there. Otherwise, it falls to the `NonGoRestfulMux`. This separation exists because API group endpoints (e.g., `/apis/apps/v1/deployments`) are registered dynamically via go-restful, while system endpoints (`/healthz`, `/metrics`, `/debug/pprof`) are registered statically on the mux.\n\n`FullHandlerChain` wraps the `director` with the full filter chain (authentication, authorization, audit, etc.). Every incoming request enters through `FullHandlerChain`.\n\n### 2.4 APIGroupInfo and API Installation\n\n**File:** `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:72`\n\n```go\ntype APIGroupInfo struct {\n    PrioritizedVersions            []schema.GroupVersion\n    VersionedResourcesStorageMap   map[string]map[string]rest.Storage\n    Scheme                         *runtime.Scheme\n    NegotiatedSerializer           runtime.NegotiatedSerializer\n    ParameterCodec                 runtime.ParameterCodec\n}\n```\n\n`VersionedResourcesStorageMap` is a two-level map: `version -> resourceName -> rest.Storage`. When an API group is installed (via `GenericAPIServer.InstallAPIGroup` or `InstallLegacyAPIGroup`), the server iterates this map and calls `APIInstaller.Install()` (`pkg/endpoints/installer.go:195`) for each version. The installer:\n\n1. Sorts resource paths deterministically\n2. For each path, calls `registerResourceHandlers()` which inspects the `rest.Storage` object to determine which interfaces it implements (`rest.Getter`, `rest.Lister`, `rest.Creater`, etc.)\n3. Creates go-restful `Route` objects for each supported HTTP verb\n4. Registers the routes on a go-restful `WebService`\n\nThis is the mechanism by which the REST verbs available for a resource are determined: if the storage object implements `rest.Updater`, PUT is registered; if it implements `rest.Watcher`, the watch endpoint is registered; and so on.\n\n### 2.5 REST Storage Interfaces\n\n**File:** `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go`\n\nThe REST layer defines fine-grained interfaces that a resource storage can implement:\n\n| Interface | Verb | Method |\n|-----------|------|--------|\n| `Getter` | GET | `Get(ctx, name, options)` |\n| `Lister` | LIST | `List(ctx, options)` |\n| `Creater` | POST | `Create(ctx, obj, validate, options)` |\n| `Updater` | PUT | `Update(ctx, name, objInfo, validate, validate, options)` |\n| `Patcher` | PATCH | (composed from Get + Update) |\n| `GracefulDeleter` | DELETE | `Delete(ctx, name, validate, options)` |\n| `CollectionDeleter` | DELETE collection | `DeleteCollection(ctx, validate, options, listOptions)` |\n| `Watcher` | WATCH | `Watch(ctx, options)` |\n| `Connecter` | CONNECT | `Connect(ctx, name, options, responder)` |\n\n`StandardStorage` aggregates `Getter`, `Lister`, `CreaterUpdater`, `GracefulDeleter`, and `Watcher` into a single convenience interface.\n\n### 2.6 Generic Registry Store\n\n**File:** `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go:100`\n\nThe `Store` struct is the standard implementation of `rest.StandardStorage`. Almost every built-in Kubernetes resource (pods, deployments, services, etc.) uses an embedded `Store`. It provides:\n\n- CRUD operations with conflict detection via `ResourceVersion`\n- Pluggable strategies: `CreateStrategy`, `UpdateStrategy`, `DeleteStrategy` (implement resource-specific validation, defaulting, canonicalization)\n- Lifecycle hooks: `BeginCreate`, `AfterCreate`, `BeginUpdate`, `AfterUpdate`, `AfterDelete`\n- `Decorator` for transforming objects after storage retrieval\n- `TTLFunc` for time-to-live eviction (used by Events)\n- `Storage` field holding a `DryRunnableStorage` that wraps the underlying `storage.Interface`\n- `DeleteCollectionWorkers` for parallel bulk deletion\n- Garbage collection integration via finalizer awareness\n\nThe Store delegates actual persistence to a `storage.Interface` (`pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`), which is typically the cacher wrapping etcd3.\n\n### 2.7 Storage Interface and etcd3 Backend\n\n**File:** `pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`\n\n```go\ntype Interface interface {\n    Versioner() Versioner\n    Create(ctx, key, obj, out, ttl) error\n    Delete(ctx, key, out, preconditions, validateDeletion, cachedExistingObject) error\n    Watch(ctx, key, opts) (watch.Interface, error)\n    Get(ctx, key, opts, objPtr) error\n    GetList(ctx, key, opts, listObj) error\n    GuaranteedUpdate(ctx, key, destination, ignoreNotFound, preconditions, tryUpdate, cachedExistingObject) error\n    Count(key) (int64, error)\n    ReadinessCheck() error\n    RequestWatchProgress(ctx) error\n}\n```\n\nThe `etcd3.store` (`pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go`) implements this interface. It:\n- Serializes objects to protobuf or JSON before writing to etcd\n- Uses etcd transactions for conflict detection (`GuaranteedUpdate` performs a compare-and-swap)\n- Manages a `leaseManager` for efficient TTL handling (multiplexes many objects onto shared etcd leases)\n- Implements `Watch` by creating etcd watchers with revision-based resumption\n- Handles encryption at rest via `value.Transformer` (wraps the codec layer)\n\n### 2.8 Watch Cache (Cacher)\n\n**File:** `pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go:80`\n\nThe `Cacher` sits between the generic registry and the etcd3 store. It implements `storage.Interface` and serves as an in-memory read cache and watch multiplexer. Key behaviors:\n\n- **Read path:** `Get` and `GetList` are served from the in-memory cache, avoiding etcd round-trips for most reads. The cache is populated by a reflector that watches etcd.\n- **Watch path:** Client watches are served from the in-memory event buffer rather than creating individual etcd watchers. This is critical for scalability -- without it, every `kubectl get pods -w` would create a separate etcd watch.\n- **Event history:** The `watchCache` (`pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go`) maintains a ring buffer of recent events (`DefaultEventFreshDuration` is about 75 seconds). Clients that reconnect with a recent `resourceVersion` can resume from this buffer without a full relist.\n- **Bookmark events:** Sent periodically (every ~60s, `defaultBookmarkFrequency`) to advance clients' `resourceVersion` without delivering actual changes, reducing the chance of `410 Gone` on reconnect.\n- **Consistency:** The cache may be slightly stale for reads. Writes always go to etcd first. A List with `resourceVersion=\"\"` (the default) is served from cache and may not reflect the most recent write. A List with `resourceVersion=\"0\"` explicitly requests the latest cached state. A Get/List with a specific `resourceVersion` will wait until the cache has caught up to that version (with a timeout).\n\nConfig fields (`cacher.go:80`):\n- `Storage` -- the underlying `storage.Interface` (etcd3 store)\n- `ResourcePrefix` -- etcd key prefix for this resource type\n- `KeyFunc`, `GetAttrsFunc` -- extract storage keys and indexable attributes\n- `IndexerFuncs` / `Indexers` -- for optimizing which watchers receive which events\n- `EventsHistoryWindow` -- minimum duration the event buffer covers\n\n### 2.9 Admission Control\n\n**File:** `pkg/admission/interfaces.go`\n\nAdmission runs after authentication and authorization but before the object is persisted. Two interfaces:\n\n```go\ntype MutationInterface interface {\n    Interface\n    Admit(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error\n}\n\ntype ValidationInterface interface {\n    Interface\n    Validate(ctx context.Context, a Attributes, o ObjectInterfaces) error\n}\n```\n\nThe `Attributes` interface (`interfaces.go:31`) provides request context to plugins: the operation type (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, CONNECT), the object, the old object (for updates), the user info, the resource, and the subresource.\n\nThe admission chain runs mutation plugins first, then validation plugins. Mutation plugins may modify the object; validation plugins must not. A request that fails any validation plugin is rejected. The chain is constructed at server startup and cannot be modified at runtime.\n\nBuilt-in plugin categories under `pkg/admission/plugin/`:\n- **webhook/** -- `mutating/` and `validating/` webhook dispatchers that call out to external admission webhooks\n- **policy/** -- ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL-based in-process validation)\n- **cel/** -- CEL compilation and evaluation\n- **namespace/** -- namespace lifecycle enforcement (e.g., rejecting operations in terminating namespaces)\n- **resourcequota/** -- resource quota enforcement\n\n### 2.10 Authentication and Authorization\n\n**Authentication** (`pkg/authentication/`):\n- `authenticator.Request` interface: `AuthenticateRequest(req *http.Request) (*Response, bool, error)`\n- Implementations: bearer token (`request/bearertoken/`), x509 client certs (`request/x509/`), anonymous (`request/anonymous/`), request header proxy (`request/headerrequest/`), websocket (`request/websocket/`)\n- Token authenticators: JWT (`token/tokenfile/`, `token/cache/`), union (tries multiple authenticators in sequence)\n- The authentication filter (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go`) calls into the configured authenticator and attaches user info to the request context\n\n**Authorization** (`pkg/authorization/`):\n- `authorizer.Authorizer` interface: `Authorize(ctx, Attributes) (Decision, reason, error)`\n- The authorization filter (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go`) checks the authorizer after authentication succeeds\n- Decisions: `DecisionAllow`, `DecisionDeny`, `DecisionNoOpinion`\n\n### 2.11 Priority and Fairness (APF)\n\n**Files:** `pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go`, `pkg/util/flowcontrol/`\n\nAPF replaces the older `MaxInFlightLimit` filter. It classifies requests into flow schemas and priority levels, then applies fair queuing and concurrency limiting. In `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`, if `FlowControl` is configured, the APF filter is used; otherwise, the simpler `MaxInFlightLimit` filter is used.\n\nAPF is significant operationally because it prevents a single misbehaving client from starving the server. Requests that exceed their queue depth receive `429 Too Many Requests` or `503 Service Unavailable` responses.\n\n---\n\n## 3. End-to-End Request Flow\n\nThis section traces a `CREATE Pod` request from the TCP connection to etcd write and response.\n\n### 3.1 TLS Termination and Handler Chain Entry\n\nThe request arrives at the TLS listener configured by `SecureServingInfo`. It enters `APIServerHandler.FullHandlerChain`, which is the outermost `http.Handler` wrapping all filters.\n\n### 3.2 Filter Chain\n\nThe filter chain is constructed by `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` (`pkg/server/config.go:1028`). Filters are applied inside-out in the source code, so the first filter listed at the bottom of the function runs first. The execution order, from outermost to innermost:\n\n1. **AuditInit** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit_init.go`) -- initializes the audit context on the request\n2. **PanicRecovery** (`pkg/server/filters/wrap.go`) -- catches panics, logs them, returns 500\n3. **MuxAndDiscoveryComplete** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/muxcomplete.go`) -- blocks requests until all API groups are installed (returns 503 during startup)\n4. **RequestInfo** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/requestinfo.go`) -- parses the URL into API group, version, resource, namespace, name, verb; attaches to context\n5. **RequestReceivedTimestamp** -- records when the request was received\n6. **LatencyTrackers** -- sets up latency tracking in the context\n7. **HTTPLogging** -- request/response logging\n8. **RetryAfter** (`pkg/server/filters/retry-after.go`) -- if the server is shutting down and `ShutdownSendRetryAfter` is enabled, returns `429` with `Retry-After` header\n9. **HSTS** -- adds Strict-Transport-Security headers\n10. **CacheControl** -- adds Cache-Control headers\n11. **GoawayChance** (`pkg/server/filters/goaway.go`) -- randomly sends GOAWAY to HTTP/2 clients to spread reconnections (avoids thundering herd on server restart)\n12. **WatchTermination** -- ensures watches are terminated during shutdown\n13. **WaitGroup** (`pkg/server/filters/waitgroup.go`) -- adds the request to the server's wait group so shutdown can wait for in-flight requests\n14. **RequestDeadline** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/request_deadline.go`) -- sets a context deadline based on the request timeout parameter\n15. **TimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests** (`pkg/server/filters/timeout.go`) -- enforces a hard timeout on non-long-running requests (watches, proxy, exec are long-running)\n16. **WarningRecorder** -- attaches a warning recorder to context for collecting API warnings\n17. **CORS** (`pkg/server/filters/cors.go`) -- handles Cross-Origin Resource Sharing\n18. **Authentication** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go`) -- calls the authenticator; on failure, returns 401\n19. **Tracing** -- OpenTelemetry distributed tracing\n20. **Audit** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/audit.go`) -- records the audit event (RequestReceived stage)\n21. **Impersonation** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/impersonation/`) -- handles user impersonation headers\n22. **FlowControl** (`pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go`) -- APF queuing and concurrency limiting (or `MaxInFlightLimit` if APF is disabled)\n23. **Authorization** (`pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go`) -- calls the authorizer; on failure, returns 403\n\n### 3.3 Director Routing\n\nAfter the filter chain, the request reaches the `director` (`pkg/server/handler.go:121`). For a `POST /api/v1/namespaces/default/pods`, the director finds this path is registered in the `GoRestfulContainer` and dispatches there.\n\n### 3.4 go-restful Routing to REST Handler\n\nThe go-restful container holds `WebService` instances registered during API installation. Each `WebService` covers a URL prefix like `/api/v1` or `/apis/apps/v1`. The go-restful router matches the request to a specific `Route`, which points to a handler function created by `registerResourceHandlers` in `pkg/endpoints/installer.go`.\n\n### 3.5 Request Handler (Create)\n\nFor a CREATE operation, the handler is `createHandler` (`pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go`). This function:\n\n1. **Decodes** the request body using the negotiated serializer (JSON, YAML, or protobuf)\n2. **Defaults** the object using the scheme's defaulting functions\n3. Calls **admission** (mutation + validation)\n4. Calls the `rest.Storage`'s `Create` method\n\n### 3.6 Generic Registry Store.Create\n\nThe `Store.Create()` method in `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go`:\n\n1. Calls `BeginCreate` hook (if set)\n2. Runs `CreateStrategy.PrepareForCreate()` -- resource-specific defaulting (e.g., setting pod status to Pending)\n3. Runs `CreateStrategy.Validate()` -- resource-specific validation\n4. Computes the storage key using `KeyFunc`\n5. Calls `Storage.Create()` on the underlying `storage.Interface`\n6. Runs `AfterCreate` hook (if set)\n7. Runs `Decorator` (if set)\n\n### 3.7 Cacher Pass-Through on Write\n\nFor write operations (Create, Update, Delete), the `Cacher` passes through directly to the underlying etcd3 store. The cacher does not cache writes -- they always go to etcd. The cacher's reflector will pick up the change asynchronously and update the in-memory cache.\n\n### 3.8 etcd3 Store.Create\n\nThe `etcd3.store.Create()` method (`pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go`):\n\n1. Serializes the object using the codec (applies `value.Transformer` for encryption at rest)\n2. Computes the etcd key (e.g., `/registry/pods/default/my-pod`)\n3. Executes an etcd `Txn` (transaction): compare that the key does not exist, then put. If the key exists, returns `AlreadyExists` error.\n4. Deserializes the response (including the new `resourceVersion` from the etcd `ModRevision`)\n5. Returns the created object with its `resourceVersion` set\n\n### 3.9 Response Serialization\n\nThe response flows back up through the handler chain. The REST handler serializes the response object using content negotiation (the client's `Accept` header determines JSON, YAML, or protobuf). The response passes back out through all filters (audit records the ResponseComplete stage, latency trackers finalize, etc.).\n\n### 3.10 Watch Delivery\n\nAny active watch on pods in the `default` namespace will be notified. The cacher's reflector observes the etcd change, adds it to the `watchCache` ring buffer, and the `cacheWatcher` goroutines (`pkg/storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go`) deliver the event to connected watchers (filtered by namespace, label selector, field selector).\n\n---\n\n## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs\n\n### 4.1 Watch Cache Consistency\n\nThe watch cache is eventually consistent. A client that creates an object and immediately lists may not see it if the list is served from cache. This is by design -- `resourceVersion=\"\"` means \"serve from cache, any version is acceptable.\"\n\n**Operational implication:** Controllers should use the `resourceVersion` from their watch events, not perform unversioned lists. If a controller must see its own write, it should read the response from the write operation, which contains the authoritative object.\n\n**Risk of `410 Gone`:** If a watcher disconnects for longer than the event buffer window (~75 seconds), and then reconnects with its last `resourceVersion`, the cacher cannot serve the missed events. It returns `410 Gone`, forcing the client to relist. Bookmark events mitigate this by advancing the client's `resourceVersion` more frequently.\n\n### 4.2 etcd Transaction Failures and Conflict Detection\n\nAll writes use etcd transactions with compare-and-swap on the `modRevision`. Concurrent writes to the same key result in one succeeding and the others receiving a `Conflict` (HTTP 409) error. Clients (including controllers) are expected to retry with exponential backoff.\n\n`GuaranteedUpdate` in etcd3 (`pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go`) retries the transaction internally if the `tryUpdate` function returns a modified object, but only a limited number of times. Sustained contention on a hot key (e.g., a frequently-updated ConfigMap) can cause write failures.\n\n### 4.3 Graceful Shutdown Sequence\n\nThe shutdown sequence (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:536`, see ASCII diagram at line 496) is multi-phase:\n\n1. **Context cancellation** triggers `ShutdownInitiated`\n2. **ShutdownDelayDuration** (default 0s, configurable) elapses \u2192 `AfterShutdownDelayDuration`. This delay exists to allow load balancers to remove the server from rotation before it stops accepting requests.\n3. **PreShutdownHooks** execute (e.g., draining leader election)\n4. Both signals above merge into `NotAcceptingNewRequest`\n5. Two wait groups drain in parallel: `NonLongRunningRequestWaitGroup` and `WatchRequestWaitGroup`\n6. Both drained \u2192 `InFlightRequestsDrained`\n7. HTTP server stop (`stopHttpServerCtx`) and audit backend shutdown happen\n8. `HTTPServerStoppedListening` fires\n\n**Operational implication:** Setting `ShutdownDelayDuration` too low in a load-balanced environment can cause client errors during rolling restarts. Too high delays node shutdown.\n\n### 4.4 Priority and Fairness vs. MaxInFlight\n\nAPF (`FlowControl`) supersedes the simpler `MaxInFlightLimit` / `MaxMutatingInFlightLimit` settings. When APF is enabled, those limits are ignored.\n\nAPF provides multi-tenant fairness: requests from different users/service-accounts are queued independently. However, APF adds memory overhead (queues), latency (queuing time), and configuration complexity (FlowSchema + PriorityLevelConfiguration CRDs).\n\n**Failure mode:** If APF is misconfigured (e.g., the `catch-all` FlowSchema has a very low concurrency share), system-critical requests (like leader election renewals) can be starved. The `exempt` priority level should always have sufficient concurrency for system requests.\n\n### 4.5 Timeout Behavior\n\n`TimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests` (`pkg/server/filters/timeout.go`) wraps non-long-running requests with a hard deadline (default: 60 seconds, from `Config.RequestTimeout`). If the handler doesn't complete in time, the filter:\n1. Writes a `504 Gateway Timeout` response\n2. Does NOT cancel the handler's context -- the handler may continue executing (and the storage operation may complete in etcd), but the client will have received the timeout error\n\n**Operational implication:** A client may receive a timeout error even though the operation succeeded in etcd. Clients must be prepared for this and should check whether the object exists before retrying creates (or use `fieldManager` for idempotent applies).\n\n### 4.6 GoAway Filter and Connection Spreading\n\nThe `GoawayChance` filter (`pkg/server/filters/goaway.go`) randomly sends HTTP/2 GOAWAY frames to clients. This exists because HTTP/2 connections are long-lived, and without it, all clients would stay pinned to the same server after a rolling restart. The filter probabilistically forces clients to reconnect, spreading load.\n\n**Tradeoff:** Setting `GoawayChance` too high causes excessive reconnection overhead. Too low, and connection spreading is ineffective after a restart.\n\n### 4.7 Admission Webhook Failures\n\nExternal admission webhooks are called over HTTPS. If a webhook is unreachable or times out:\n- `failurePolicy: Fail` (default for validating webhooks) -- the request is rejected\n- `failurePolicy: Ignore` -- the request proceeds without the webhook's check\n\n**Operational implication:** A misconfigured validating webhook with `failurePolicy: Fail` that matches all resources (including its own namespace) can deadlock the cluster: the webhook's own pod cannot be updated because the webhook is down.\n\n### 4.8 Storage Versioning and Forward/Backward Compatibility\n\nObjects are stored in etcd at a specific \"storage version\" (the version used to serialize to etcd). When the storage version changes across upgrades, old objects remain in the old format until rewritten. The `StorageVersionMigrator` handles this.\n\nThe `EffectiveVersion` and `EmulationForwardCompatible` fields on `Config` control which API versions are served. This allows a newer binary to emulate an older version's API surface.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Extension Points, Hooks, and Their Risks\n\n### 5.1 BuildHandlerChainFunc\n\n**Location:** `Config.BuildHandlerChainFunc` (`pkg/server/config.go:186`)\n\nThis function signature is `func(apiHandler http.Handler, c *Config) http.Handler`. It completely controls the filter chain. Setting a custom function replaces the entire default chain.\n\n**Use case:** Adding custom middleware (e.g., request logging, custom rate limiting, header injection).\n\n**Risk:** Omitting any default filter (e.g., authentication, authorization) would be a security vulnerability. If you override this, you must replicate or call `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` and wrap its result.\n\n### 5.2 PostStartHooks\n\n**Location:** `GenericAPIServer.postStartHooks`, registered via `AddPostStartHook()` or `AddPostStartHookOrDie()`\n\nPostStartHooks run asynchronously after the HTTP listener starts. They receive a `PostStartHookContext` containing a `LoopbackClientConfig` and `StopCh`.\n\n**Built-in hooks (examples):**\n- `start-informers` -- starts shared informers\n- `priority-and-fairness-config-consumer` -- bootstraps APF configuration\n- `storage-object-count-tracker-hook` -- tracks object counts for APF\n\n**Use case:** Initializing controllers, waiting for caches to sync, registering API discovery info.\n\n**Risk:** A hook that blocks forever will prevent the server from reporting as ready (the readyz check for PostStartHooks will fail). Hooks should respect `StopCh` and not deadlock.\n\n### 5.3 PreShutdownHooks\n\nRun during graceful shutdown. Used for draining leader elections, closing connections to external systems.\n\n### 5.4 Admission Plugins\n\n**Registration:** `pkg/admission/` provides `Register()` for in-process plugins, and the webhook infrastructure for out-of-process plugins.\n\n**Extension model:** You can add custom in-process admission plugins by implementing `MutationInterface` and/or `ValidationInterface` and registering them in the admission chain at startup.\n\n**Risk:** A slow or panicking admission plugin can block or crash request processing. Mutation plugins that make external calls add latency to every write.\n\n### 5.5 REST Storage Interfaces (Custom Resources)\n\n**Location:** `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go`\n\nAny type that implements `rest.Storage` (and optionally `rest.Getter`, `rest.Lister`, etc.) can be registered as an API resource. This is how CRDs work: the apiextensions-apiserver creates dynamic `rest.Storage` implementations backed by unstructured storage.\n\n**Extension model:** To add a custom resource type without CRDs, implement the relevant `rest.` interfaces and register via `APIGroupInfo.VersionedResourcesStorageMap`.\n\n### 5.6 Store Strategy Hooks\n\n**Location:** `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go`\n\nThe `Store` struct exposes several hook points:\n- `CreateStrategy` / `UpdateStrategy` / `DeleteStrategy` -- control validation, defaulting, field clearing\n- `BeginCreate` / `BeginUpdate` -- \"transaction-like\" hooks that return a `FinishFunc` called on completion\n- `AfterCreate` / `AfterUpdate` / `AfterDelete` -- post-operation hooks\n- `Decorator` -- transforms objects before returning to the client (but decorated fields are not watchable)\n\n**Risk:** `BeginCreate`/`BeginUpdate` hooks that return errors prevent the operation. `Decorator` modifications are invisible to watch clients, potentially causing confusing behavior if the decorated data is significant.\n\n### 5.7 Storage Decorators and Encryption\n\n**Location:** `pkg/storage/value/` (encryption transformers), `pkg/server/options/etcd.go` (decorator construction)\n\nStorage decorators wrap the `storage.Interface` to add behavior. The primary decorator is the `Cacher`. Encryption at rest is implemented as a `value.Transformer` applied within the etcd3 store's codec layer.\n\n**Extension model:** Custom `value.Transformer` implementations can add encryption providers (e.g., KMS, AES-CBC, AES-GCM, Secretbox).\n\n### 5.8 Authentication and Authorization Plugins\n\n- Custom `authenticator.Request` implementations can be composed using the union authenticator\n- Custom `authorizer.Authorizer` implementations are set on `Config.Authorization.Authorizer`\n- RBAC, ABAC, webhook, and node authorizers are the built-in implementations\n\n### 5.9 LifecycleSignals\n\n**Location:** `pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go`\n\nSignals like `ShutdownInitiated`, `MuxAndDiscoveryComplete`, `InFlightRequestsDrained`, etc. are `<-chan struct{}` channels. External code can wait on these to coordinate with server lifecycle.\n\n---\n\n## 6. File and Directory Map\n\n### Core Server\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/server/genericapiserver.go` | GenericAPIServer struct, DelegationTarget, PrepareRun, RunWithContext, lifecycle management |\n| `pkg/server/config.go` | Config, RecommendedConfig, CompletedConfig, DefaultBuildHandlerChain |\n| `pkg/server/handler.go` | APIServerHandler, director routing between GoRestful and NonGoRestful mux |\n| `pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go` | Shutdown lifecycle signal definitions |\n| `pkg/server/hooks.go` | PostStartHook and PreShutdownHook registration |\n| `pkg/server/healthz/` | Health check registration and handlers |\n\n### Request Filters\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authentication.go` | Authentication filter |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/authorization.go` | Authorization filter |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/audit.go` | Audit logging filter |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/impersonation/` | User impersonation |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/requestinfo.go` | URL parsing into API request attributes |\n| `pkg/endpoints/filters/request_deadline.go` | Per-request deadline enforcement |\n| `pkg/server/filters/maxinflight.go` | Simple max-in-flight concurrency limiter |\n| `pkg/server/filters/priority-and-fairness.go` | APF concurrency limiter |\n| `pkg/server/filters/timeout.go` | Non-long-running request timeout |\n| `pkg/server/filters/cors.go` | CORS handling |\n| `pkg/server/filters/goaway.go` | HTTP/2 GOAWAY for connection spreading |\n| `pkg/server/filters/waitgroup.go` | In-flight request tracking for shutdown |\n| `pkg/server/filters/wrap.go` | Panic recovery |\n\n### Endpoint Registration\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/endpoints/installer.go` | APIInstaller -- maps rest.Storage interfaces to HTTP routes |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/create.go` | CREATE request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/update.go` | UPDATE request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/delete.go` | DELETE request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/get.go` | GET/LIST request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/watch.go` | WATCH request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/patch.go` | PATCH request handler |\n| `pkg/endpoints/handlers/fieldmanager/` | Server-side apply field management |\n\n### REST and Registry\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/registry/rest/rest.go` | All REST storage interfaces (Getter, Lister, Creater, etc.) |\n| `pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go` | Generic Store implementing StandardStorage |\n| `pkg/registry/generic/storage_decorator.go` | Storage decorator construction (cacher wrapping) |\n\n### Storage\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/storage/interfaces.go` | storage.Interface -- the low-level storage contract |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go` | etcd3 implementation of storage.Interface |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/watcher.go` | etcd3 watch implementation |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/compact.go` | etcd compaction |\n| `pkg/storage/etcd3/lease_manager.go` | TTL lease management |\n| `pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go` | In-memory watch cache and read cache |\n| `pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go` | Ring buffer for watch events |\n| `pkg/storage/cacher/cache_watcher.go` | Per-client watch event delivery |\n| `pkg/storage/value/` | Encryption at rest transformers |\n\n### Admission\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/admission/interfaces.go` | MutationInterface, ValidationInterface, Attributes |\n| `pkg/admission/chain.go` | Admission chain execution |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/webhook/mutating/` | Mutating webhook dispatcher |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/webhook/validating/` | Validating webhook dispatcher |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/policy/` | ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (CEL) |\n| `pkg/admission/plugin/namespace/` | Namespace lifecycle admission |\n\n### Authentication and Authorization\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `pkg/authentication/authenticator/interfaces.go` | authenticator.Request interface |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/bearertoken/` | Bearer token authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/x509/` | Client certificate authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/headerrequest/` | Request header (proxy) authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/request/anonymous/` | Anonymous authentication |\n| `pkg/authentication/token/cache/` | Token authentication response caching |\n| `pkg/authorization/authorizer/interfaces.go` | authorizer.Authorizer interface |\n\n### kube-apiserver Wiring (outside the library, in the consumer)\n\n| Path | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go` | CreateServerChain -- builds the three-layer delegation |\n| `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/aggregator.go` | Aggregator layer configuration |\n| `pkg/controlplane/instance.go` | Built-in API group registration (all RESTStorageProviders) |\n| `pkg/controlplane/apiserver/server.go` | Control plane server with PostStartHooks |\n\nAll paths above are relative to `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/` unless they start with `cmd/` or `pkg/controlplane/`, which are at the repository root.\n",
  "filePath": "/workspace/documentation.md",
  "originalFile": null,
  "structuredPatch": [],
  "type": "create"
}

1. WRITE /workspace/documentation.md
# Kubernetes API Server Architecture and Extension Guide

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

### What the apiserver library is

The `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/` library provides a reusable framework for building Kubernetes-style API servers. It is not the kube-apiserver itself; rather, kube-apiserver is one consumer of this library. The library handles HTTP serving, request routing, authentication, authorization, admission control, storage abstraction, watch/notification, content negotiation, API discovery, and graceful lifecycle management.

Any Go program that needs to serve a Kubernetes-compatible REST API (with support for watches, server-side apply, declarative validation, etc.) can import this library and build on it. The `apiextensions-apiserver` (serving CRDs) and `kube-aggregator` (the aggregation layer) are two other major consumers that ship in the same binary as kube-apiserver.

### Boundary: what lives inside vs. outside

**Inside `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`:**
- Generic server lifecycle (`pkg/server/`)
- Request filter chain (authn, authz, audit, flow control, timeout, etc.)
- REST endpoint installation and HTTP verb mapping (`pkg/endpoints/`)
- Storage abstraction and etcd3 backend (`pkg/storage/`)
- Watch cache (`pkg/storage/cacher/`)
- Admission control interfaces and built-in plugins (`pkg/admission/`)
- Authentication and authorization interfaces (`pkg/authentication/`, `pkg/authorization/`)
- OpenAPI schema generation and serving (`pkg/endpoints/openapi/`)
- Server-sent encryption at rest (`pkg/storage/value/`)

**Outside (upstream dependencies):**
- `k8s.io/apimachinery` -- runtime types, scheme, serialization, versioning, field selectors, labels. The apiserver library never modifies apimachinery types.
- `k8s.io/client-go
... (truncated)
